general: Do not routinely suction mouth but suction only if there is something (e.g., thick meconium) to suck out
Dry and wrap up, preferably in a dry pre-warmed soft towel
Delayed cord clamping - clamping the umbilical cord after 1 minute is recommended for all normal births except in intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), infants of diabetic mothers and asphyxia
Hand the baby to the mother for her to put immediately to breast

medications.dose: Instill into both eyes once only

diseases.caseDefinition: Suspected or confirmed infection in newborn

majorCriteria: Confirmed sepsis or chorioamnionitis in mother
Confirmed or suspected sepsis in twin
Severe Respiratory Distress in a term infant
minorCriteria: Antenatal: ROM >18h, Spontaneous Preterm Birth, GBS sepsis in previous baby or documented GBS carriage in this pregnancy (urine or vaginal swab)
Natal: Born Before Arrival, Meconium Stained Liquor

Postnatal: Respiratory distress not obviously related to environmental hypothermia or delayed transition to extra-uterine life, Hypoxia, Apnoea, Hypoglycaemia/Hyperglycaemia not otherwise explained, Temperature instability not explained by environmental factors, Acidosis not obviously related to HIE, Unexplained bleeding or thrombocytopenia, Mild encephalopathy/Altered responsiveness, Altered tone not otherwise explained, Feed intolerance/feeding difficulty, Abnormal heart rate (<90 or >160), Jaundice in first 24h, Signs of shock
diagnosticCriteria.indications: Any Major Criteria OR two or more Minor Criteria → do blood culture and start antibiotics; One minor criterion present → observe for 12h, start antibiotics if not feeding well, has respiratory distress or appears lethargic/sick

alternative.note: Can be used if gentamicin unavailable

gentamicinDosage.lessThan1000g: 5mg/kg once every 48 hours for first two weeks, then once every 36 hours
gentamicinDosage.1000gTo2000g: 5mg/kg, less than 1 week old: once every 48 hours; >1 week old: once every 36 hours
gentamicinDosage.moreThan2000g: 4mg/kg, less than 1 week old: once every 24 hours; 1 week and above: once every 24-48 hours

diseases.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical suspicion of meningitis in neonate

alternatives.note: Can be used if benzyl penicillin not available
Can be used as alternative for meningitis

diseases.caseDefinition: Inflammation and necrosis of bowel in newborn

general: Give nothing by mouth
Supportive care is vital: oxygen, intravenous fluids, warmth, and nasogastric continuous drainage
Anticipate complications such as bleeding, vomiting, perforation, seizures
Refer for specialist diagnosis and care
The important principle in treating these babies is minimal handling

urgentReferral: Jaundice developing within 24 hours of birth → refer to unit capable of performing exchange transfusion
Jaundiced babies who look ill → refer
diagnosticCriteria.wellBabies: Jaundice developing in well babies may be treated with phototherapy

phototherapy.ifUnavailable: Expose to the sun intermittently for a maximum of two hours (keep warm), shade the baby's eyes with a loose-fitting bandage over cotton wool pads. Continue until the baby is no longer yellow
phototherapy.additional: Give an extra 20ml/kg/24hrs of fluid. Be very careful that the baby does not get cold (or hot). Encourage increased breastfeeding

management.serumBilirubin: Where possible check the serum bilirubin levels

management.hypoglycaemiaCheck: Always check for hypoglycaemia. If dextrose <2.2mmol/l (45mg%) immediately give:

hypoglycaemiaTreatment.dose: 4ml/kg per hour OR 2ml/kg given as a bolus followed by continuous infusion of dextrose at 6-8mg/kg/minute
1ml/kg diluted with equal quantity of water for injection

management.ifIvImpossible: If intravenous route is impossible give breast milk through nasogastric route - 10-20ml/kg initially and continue normal requirement two hourly. Dextrose should not be given by nasogastric tube. If blood glucose cannot be measured give empirical treatment with glucose.
management.generalManagement: ABC: Manage Airways and Breathing (have bag and mask ready); Ensure Circulatory access

anticonvulsants.loadingDose: 20mg per kg, repeat in 30 minutes if still convulsing, add dose of 10mg/kg every 30 minutes until a maximum dose of 40mg/kg
anticonvulsants.note: Drug of choice for neonatal seizures

anticonvulsants.loadingDose: 20mg/kg iv, repeat dose of 10mg/kg may be repeated in refractory seizures
anticonvulsants.note: Indicated when maximum dose of phenobarbitone 40mg/kg fails to resolve seizures or adverse effects like respiratory depression, bradycardia or hypotension occur; Dilute phenytoin in Normal saline - Do Not Use Dextrose

benzodiazepines.note: May be required in 15-20% of neonatal seizures

medications.dose: 0.05mg/kg iv bolus over 2-5 minutes (may be repeated)
0.15mg/kg iv bolus followed by an infusion of 0.1-0.4mg/kg/hr

benzodiazepines.warning: Avoid diazepam in neonatal seizures because of its short duration of anti-epileptic effect and its very prolonged sedative effect

additionalMeasures: Perform lumbar puncture to rule out any CNS infection and start meningitis treatment while waiting for results
Check blood levels of calcium, magnesium
If there is hypocalcaemia give 2mls/kg of 10% Calcium Gluconate

general: Minimal handling
Supplemental Oxygen with nasal prongs, Maintain Oxygen saturation 90-94%
Use Continuous Positive Airways Pressure (CPAP) ventilation if available
Initially no oral feeds if severe respiratory distress
Intravenous fluids (see section on fluids)
Treat for neonatal sepsis with antibiotics (see section on NNS)
Maintain normal temperature range by Kangaroo Mother Care or using an incubator if available
If respiratory distress persists do a chest x-ray to rule out pneumothorax

surfactant.repetition: Dose may be repeated within 48 hours at intervals of at least 6 hours for up to 4 doses

prevention.indication: For pregnant mothers <34/40 and at risk of premature delivery

prevention.repetition: Dose may be repeated within 48 hours at intervals of at least 6 hours for up to 4 doses

diseases.caseDefinition: Apnoea due to immaturity of respiratory centre in premature infants

prevention.note: If caffeine citrate is not available give loading dose of aminophylline at 6mg/kg iv over 20 minutes followed by maintenance dose of 2.5mg/kg every 12 hours

monitoring: Use an apnoea monitor if available or a Pulse oximeter with alarm turned on for hypoxaemia

management.general: Normal newborn babies do not require any long-term vitamin or mineral supplementation
management.prematureInfants: Those babies born at <36 weeks gestation and/or <1.5kg should be given from age 2 weeks:
management.ironSupplementation: Starting from the age of one month:

diseases.caseDefinition: Signs indicating severe illness in child

ask: If the child is not able to drink or breastfeed
If the child is vomiting everything
If the child has had convulsions
If there are periods of not breathing
look: If the child is lethargic or unconscious

diseases.management: A child with any general danger sign needs urgent attention

diseases.caseDefinition: Lower respiratory tract infection in child

diagnosticCriteria.chestIndrawing: When the lower part of the chest moves in when the child breathes in
diagnosticCriteria.grunting: A soft short sound that the infant makes when breathing out

severePneumonia.classification: Any general danger sign OR chest indrawing OR stridor in a calm child
treatment: Give first dose of an appropriate antibiotic (Benzylpenicillin and gentamycin)
Treat to prevent low blood sugar (see below)
Give an appropriate antibiotic for 5 days
Advise mother to return immediately if condition worsens

noPneumonia.classification: No signs of pneumonia or of very severe disease
treatment: If coughing more than 21 days, refer for assessment
Advise mother to return immediately if condition worsens
Follow-up in 7 days if not improving

severePneumoniaMedications.note: Paediatric dose starts at 2 months in IMNCI. For babies 1-2 months see neonatal doses Table 3.5
severePneumoniaMedications.wellNourished: Well-nourished children over 6 months with severe pneumonia can be managed with benzylpenicillin only
severePneumoniaMedications.note2: Change to oral amoxicillin when patient improves and tolerates oral drugs
severePneumoniaMedications.ifLessThan6Months: If less than 6 months add high dose cotrimoxazole for 21 days and check HIV status. In HIV positive children consider steroids

lowBloodSugarPrevention: If the child is able to breastfeed ask the mother to breast feed the child

If the child cannot breastfeed but is able to swallow give expressed breast milk or a breast milk substitute. If neither are available give sugar water = 4 level teaspoons sugar (20gm) in 200ml clean water
If the child is not able to swallow, give 50ml of milk or sugar water by nasogastric tube
supportiveMeasures.airway: Nasal suction (or normal saline nasal drops) to clear the airway

painAndFever.note: Do not give paracetamol to children under 3 months of age due to liver immaturity, if indicated give cautiously

homeCareAdvice: Maintain nutrition by giving easy-to-digest high-energy food 5-7 times a day
management.followUpAdvice: Advise mother to return with the child in 2 days for re-assessment, or earlier if the child is getting worse: increased difficulty in breathing, increased difficulty in drinking, increased respiratory rate. If the child returns with any of these, refer

diseases.diagnosticCriteria: Cough or cold without pneumonia signs

management.general: Home care and instructions on when to return are all that are needed. No antibiotics, antihistamines or cough mixtures are required
homeCare: Maintain nutrition by giving easy-to-digest high-energy food 5-7 times a day
management.followUp: Advise mother/caregiver to return with the child in 2 days for reassessment, or earlier if the child is getting worse: breathing becomes difficult, child is not able to drink, breathing becomes fast, child seems worse. If the child returns with any of these, reassess

diagnosticCriteria.youngInfant: In a young infant below 2 months, wheeze is a sign of serious illness - refer
diagnosticCriteria.bronchiolitis: An infant between 2 months and 12 months may wheeze because of bronchiolitis, usually viral infection. If a child with bronchiolitis is breathing fast, refer. If not, give home care
diagnosticCriteria.asthma: In a child more than one-year wheezing may be due to asthma. If it is the first episode refer. If this child is in distress, give a rapid-acting bronchodilator for example salbutamol and refer

ifChestIndrawingOrDangerSignsOrFastBreat: Give first dose of benzyl penicillin

ifChestIndrawingOrDangerSigns: Give rapid-acting bronchodilator, oral prednisolone and antibiotic
ifFastBreathing: Nebulise with salbutamol or give oral bronchodilator
ifNoFastBreathing: Nebulise with salbutamol or give oral bronchodilator
Send home on treatment as "no pneumonia, cough/cold"

ifChestIndrawingOrDangerSigns: Nebulise with salbutamol or give oral bronchodilator
ifFastBreathing: Nebulise with salbutamol or give oral bronchodilator
ifNoFastBreathing: Nebulise with salbutamol or give oral bronchodilator
Send home on treatment as "no pneumonia, cough/cold"

previousEpisodesOver1Year.startWith: Give a rapid acting bronchodilator. Assess the child's condition 30 minutes later and treat according to this assessment
ifChestIndrawingOrDangerSigns: Give first dose of antibiotic and prednisolone
ifNoFastBreathing: Send home on treatment as "no pneumonia, cough/cold"

management.note: If asthma is suspected refer to Asthma section for detailed management

diseases.caseDefinition: Harsh noise made when a child breathes in

diagnosticCriteria.mildCroup: Stridor present only when upset. Likely to be of viral origin. An antibiotic is not required. Home care
diagnosticCriteria.severeCroup: Stridor in a calm child at rest with chest indrawing

severeCroup: Refer to higher centre of care
Do not examine the throat in case it's Epiglottitis!
If referral not possible or there is a delay give chloramphenicol or ceftriaxone and cloxacillin
management.epiglottitisSuspicion: Suspect Epiglottitis if child very ill, toxic and drooling saliva
additional: Watch carefully for signs of obstruction. Intubation or a tracheostomy may be required (poor air entry; severe chest indrawing, restlessness, pallor)
NB. Remember cyanosis is a very late sign

diseases.diagnosticCriteria: Common in age 1-2 years: sudden onset (choking); sometimes local wheeze and/or decreased air entry. May cause stridor/cough; there is usually a history that suggests inhalation of foreign body
management: Admit for bronchoscopy to remove the foreign body
Use antibiotics if there is fast breathing (secondary infection)
Whenever Foreign Body is suspected consult cardiothoracic surgeons

diseases.caseDefinition: Care for child with ear problems
diseases.diagnosticCriteria: Child with ear problem (see also Chapter on Ear, Nose and Throat Disorders)

drainingEarAdvice: Not to leave anything in the ear, such as cotton wool, between wicking treatments
Not to put oil or any fluid into the ear
Not to let the child go swimming or get water in the ear

general: Give first dose of antibiotics
Paracetamol for pain
Refer to hospital

diseases.diagnosticCriteria: Pus is seen draining from the ear and discharge is reported for less than 14 days; or ear pain
